name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تَنصُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ (٧)
```

```english
O you who have faith! If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady. (7)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَعْسًا لَّهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٨)
```

```english
As for the faithless, their lot will be to fall [into ruin], and He will render their works fruitless. (8)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٩)
```

```english
That is because they loathed what Allah has sent down, so He made their works fail. (9)
```

```arabic
۞ أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ دَمَّرَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْثَـٰلُهَا (١٠)
```

```english
Have they not travelled through the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? Allah destroyed them, and a similar [fate] awaits these faithless. (10)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَأَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ (١١)
```

```english
That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector. (11)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَمَا تَأْكُلُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ وَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ (١٢)
```

```english
Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. As for the faithless, they enjoy and eat like the cattle do, and the Fire will be their [final] abode. (12)
```

```arabic
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ هِىَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِّن قَرْيَتِكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَا نَاصِرَ لَهُمْ (١٣)
```

```english
How many a town We have destroyed which was more powerful than your town which expelled you, and they had no helper. (13)
```

```arabic
أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ كَمَن زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم (١٤)
```

```english
Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their conduct is made to seem decorous and who follow their desires? (14)
```

```arabic
مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ فِيهَآ أَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن مَّآءٍ غَيْرِ ءَاسِنٍ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن لَّبَنٍ لَّمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُۥ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ خَمْرٍ لَّذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ عَسَلٍ مُّصَفًّى ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ كَمَنْ هُوَ خَـٰلِدٌ فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَسُقُوا۟ مَآءً حَمِيمًا فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعَآءَهُمْ (١٥)
```

```english
A description of the paradise promised to the Godwary: therein are streams of unstaling water and streams of milk unchanging in flavour, and streams of wine delicious to the drinkers, and streams of purified honey; there will be every kind of fruit in it for them, and forgiveness from their Lord. [Are such ones] like those who abide in the Fire and are given to drink boiling water which cuts up their bowels? (15)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses continue in the same context as the preceding ones.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنْ تَنْصُرُوا اللهَ يَنْصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدامَكُمْ** "O you who have faith! If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady" (47:7). This is an incitement for them to engage in struggle (الجهاد) — *the striving in the way of Allah* — and a promise of victory for them if they help Allah, the Exalted. What is meant by their helping Allah is that they struggle in the way of Allah (سبيل الله) such that they fight for the sake of Allah, to support His religion and to exalt the word of truth, not to seek superiority on earth, to acquire spoils of war, or to display gallantry and courage.

What is meant by Allah’s helping them is His facilitating the causes that necessitate their emergence and victory over their enemy, such as casting terror into the hearts of the disbelievers, turning the tide of fortune in favor of the believers against them, and strengthening the hearts of the believers and emboldening them. Accordingly, the coupling of "making the feet steady" with "help" is an instance of coupling the specific with the general. The specific mention of making the feet steady — which is an allusion to emboldening and strengthening the hearts — is due to it being among the most manifest forms of help.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَتَعْساً لَهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمالَهُمْ** "As for the faithless, may they be wretched, and He has let their deeds go astray" (47:8). He mentions what is done to the disbelievers immediately after mentioning what is done for the believers who help Allah, in order to contrast their respective conditions.

Wretchedness (التعس) is for a person to fall on his face and remain upon it; its opposite is recovery (الانتعاش), which is to rise after falling on one's face. Thus, His saying: **فَتَعْساً لَهُمْ** "may they be wretched" means: may they fall into wretchedness. This is a form of invocation against them, similar to His saying: **قاتَلَهُمُ اللهُ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ** "May Allah assail them! Where do they stray?" (9:30), and **قُتِلَ الْإِنْسانُ ما أَكْفَرَهُ** "Perish man! How ungrateful is he!" (80:17). It is also possible that it is an informative statement regarding their wretchedness and the futility of the effects of their efforts by way of allusion, for a human being is at his most helpless when he has fallen upon his face.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمالَهُمْ** "That is because they loathed what Allah has sent down, so He has let their deeds go to waste" (47:9). What is meant by "what Allah has sent down" is the Quran, the laws, and the rulings that Allah, the Exalted, sent down to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and commanded obedience to and submission to; yet they loathed them and were too arrogant to follow them.

The verse provides the reason for the content of the preceding verse, and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ دَمَّرَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ أَمْثالُها** "Have they not travelled through the land and seen how was the fate of those who were before them? Allah destroyed them, and for the faithless there will be the like of it" (47:10). Destruction (التدمير) means annihilation. It is said: "Allah destroyed him" (دمره الله) meaning He annihilated him. And it is said: "Allah brought destruction upon him" (دمر الله عليه) meaning He annihilated everything pertaining to him, including his person, family, house, and property. Thus, "brought destruction upon him" (دمر عليه) is more emphatic than "destroyed him" (دمره), as has been noted. The pronoun in "the like of it" (أَمْثالُها) refers to the "fate" (العاقبة) or the "punishment" (العقوبة) implied by the preceding discourse.

What is meant by "the faithless" (الكافرين) are those who disbelieved in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The meaning is: and for those who disbelieve in you, O Muhammad, there shall be the likes of that fate or punishment. They were threatened with the "likes" (أمثال) of the fate or punishment — even though only one such fate will actually befall them — because they are exposed to many worldly and otherworldly punishments, even if only some of them actually occur. It is also possible that "the faithless" refers to disbelievers in general, making the sentence a statement of a universal principle.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ** "That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful, and because the faithless have no protector" (47:11). The word "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to what was previously mentioned regarding the helping of the believers, the detestation of the disbelievers, and their evil fate. No heed should be paid to the view that it refers to the confirmation of the fate or punishment of past nations for these people, nor to the view that it refers only to the helping of the believers; this is because the verse addresses the state of both groups: the believers and the disbelievers together.

The term "protector" (المولى) is as if it were a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) used in an adjectival sense, so it carries the meaning of "guardian" (الولي). For this reason, it is applied to the master and owner of a slave because he possesses the authority of disposal (ولاية التصرف) over the affairs of his slave. It is also applied to the helper (الناصر) because he takes charge of the affairs of the one he helps through strengthening and support. Allah, the Glorified, is the "Protector" (المولى) because He is the Owner who takes charge of the affairs of His creation in the path of the ontological creation (التكوين) and manages them as He wills. He, the Exalted, said: **ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا شَفِيعٍ** "You have no guardian or intercessor besides Him" (32:4), and He said: **وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ** "and they will be returned to Allah, their true master" (10:30). He, the Exalted, is also the "Protector" (المولى) because He takes charge of managing the affairs of His servants in the path of felicity (السعادة), guiding them toward their bliss and Paradise, granting them success in righteous deeds (الصالحات), and helping them against their enemies. This second sense of "protection" (المولوية) is exclusive to the believers, for they are the ones who have entered the fold of servitude and follow what their Lord desires of them, unlike the disbelievers.

The believers have a Protector and Guardian (الولي), who is Allah, the Glorified, as He said: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "That is because Allah is the protector of the faithful," and He said: **اللهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "Allah is the guardian of the faithful" (2:257). As for the disbelievers, they have taken idols or the lords of the idols as guardians; thus, they are their guardians according to their own claim. As such, He said, based on their false claim and by way of sarcasm: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ** "As for the faithless, their patrons are the Rebels" (2:257). However, He negated their guardianship based on the reality of the matter, saying: **وَأَنَّ الْكافِرِينَ لا مَوْلى لَهُمْ** "and because the faithless have no protector." Then He negated their guardianship absolutely, both ontologically and legislatively, saying: **أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُّ** "Have they taken guardians besides Him? It is Allah who is the Guardian" (42:9), and He said: **إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ** "These are but names which you have coined—you and your fathers" (53:23).

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: His helping the believers, His making their feet steady, His forsaking the disbelievers, His letting their deeds go astray, and His punishing them is all because He, the Exalted, is the Protector and Guardian of the believers, and because the disbelievers have no protector to help them, guide their deeds, or save them from His punishment.

From what has preceded, the weakness of the view that "Protector" (المولى) in this verse means "Helper" (الناصر) to the exclusion of "Owner" (المالك) becomes clear; otherwise, it would contradict His saying: **وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ** "and they will be returned to Allah, their true master" (10:30). The reason for this weakness is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَما تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعامُ وَالنَّارُ مَثْوىً لَهُمْ** "Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith and do righteous deeds into gardens with streams running in them. As for the faithless, they enjoy themselves and eat like the cattle eat, and the Fire will be their abode" (47:12). This is a comparison between the two groups and an exposition of the effect of Allah’s guardianship (ولاية) over the believers and the lack of it for the disbelievers in terms of the final outcome and the Hereafter: that the believers enter Paradise while the disbelievers reside in the Fire.

The discourse points to the origin of this mentioned effect by describing each of the two groups in a manner suited to their ultimate state. He points to the attribute of the believers by His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ** "those who have faith and do righteous deeds," and to the attribute of the disbelievers by His saying: **يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَما تَأْكُلُ الْأَنْعامُ** "they enjoy themselves and eat like the cattle eat." These two descriptions, through their mutual contrast, convey that the believers are rightly guided in their worldly life and have attained the truth, as they believed in Allah and performed righteous deeds, thereby following the path of rectitude and fulfilling the duty of humanity. As for the disbelievers, they have no concern for attaining the truth, nor are their hearts attached to the duties of humanity; rather, their sole concern is their bellies and their private parts. They enjoy themselves in their brief worldly life and eat as cattle eat, having no aspiration other than that and no goal beyond it.

These — the believers — are under the guardianship of Allah, as they follow a path their Lord desires for them and to which He guides them; therefore, He admits them in the Hereafter into gardens with streams running in them. Those — the disbelievers — have no guardian and have been left to themselves; therefore, their abode and residence is the Fire.

The admission of the believers into the gardens is attributed to Allah Himself, whereas the residence of the disbelievers in the Fire is not, in fulfillment of the right of the aforementioned guardianship. For He, the Exalted, has a special concern for His friends (أولياء); as for those who have stripped themselves of His guardianship, He does not care in which valley they perish.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَأَيِّنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ هِيَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِنْ قَرْيَتِكَ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ فَلا ناصِرَ لَهُمْ** "How many a town was more powerful than your town which expelled you, and We destroyed them, and they had no helper" (47:13). What is meant by "town" (القرية) is the people of the town, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: **أَهْلَكْناهُمْ** "We destroyed them," etc. The town that expelled him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is Mecca.

In this verse is a strengthening of the heart of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, a threat to the people of Mecca, and a belittling of their power: that Allah destroyed many towns, each of which was more powerful than their town, and they had no helper to assist them.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ وَاتَّبَعُوا أَهْواءَهُمْ** "Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord like those to whom the evil of their deeds has been made to seem fair and who follow their low desires?" (47:14). The context, which proceeds by comparing the state of the believers with that of the disbelievers, indicates that those who "stand on a manifest proof from their Lord" are the believers. What is meant by their being on a manifest proof (بينة) — *a clear evidence that necessitates certainty* — from their Lord is that they possess a clear indication from their Lord that necessitates certainty regarding what they believe, which is the demonstrative proof (الحجة البرهانية). Thus, they only follow conclusive proof, as is befitting for a human being whose nature is to employ reason and follow the truth.

As for those who disbelieve, they have been captivated by their evil deeds which Satan made to seem fair to them, and to which their low desires (أهواء) became attached, so they committed sins. How great is the difference between the two groups!

His saying, the Exalted: **مَثْلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ** "A description of the paradise promised to the God-conscious..." (47:15) until the end of the verse. It distinguishes between the two groups by explaining the outcome of their affairs. In reality, it is an elucidation of what was previously mentioned in His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ يُدْخِلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "Indeed Allah will admit those who have faith..." etc., regarding the difference between them. Thus, this verse is effectively a detailed exposition of that verse.

His saying: **مَثَلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ** "A description of the paradise promised to the God-conscious." The word "description" (المثل) here means "attribute" (الصفة) — as has been said — meaning the attribute of the Paradise that Allah promised to admit the God-conscious (المتقين) — *those who possess godwariness and protect themselves from Allah's displeasure* — into. Perhaps "description" (المثل) is taken in its well-known sense of "parable," from which it is understood that Paradise is too sublime and exalted for any description to encompass or for any words to define; rather, it is brought closer to the minds through parables, as alluded to in His saying: **فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ** "No one knows what has been kept hidden for them of the delight of the eyes" (32:17).

In this verse, His saying in the previous verse: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ** "those who have faith and do righteous deeds" has been replaced by His saying: **الْمُتَّقُونَ** "the God-conscious." This is a replacement of the consequent by the antecedent, for godwariness (تقوى) toward Allah necessitates faith in Him and the performance of righteous deeds.

His saying: **فِيها أَنْهارٌ مِنْ ماءٍ غَيْرِ آسِنٍ** "in it are rivers of water unstaling" (47:15), meaning it does not change by remaining for a long time. His saying: **وَأَنْهارٌ مِنْ لَبَنٍ لَمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُ** "and rivers of milk whose taste does not change," unlike the milks of this world. His saying: **وَأَنْهارٌ مِنْ خَمْرٍ لَذَّةٍ لِلشَّارِبِينَ** "and rivers of wine delicious to the drinkers." The word "delicious" (لذة) is either a feminine adjectival noun describing the wine, or an infinitive used as an adjective for emphasis, or it implies a suppressed possessive, meaning "possessing deliciousness." His saying: **وَأَنْهارٌ مِنْ عَسَلٍ مُصَفًّى** "and rivers of honey clarified," meaning pure from wax, froth, impurities, and all other annoyances and defects found in the honey of this world. His saying: **وَلَهُمْ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ** "and in it they will have every kind of fruit," using the plural for universality.

His saying: **وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ** "and forgiveness from their Lord," by which every sin and evil deed is erased from them, so their life is not clouded by any distress nor spoiled by any annoyance. In the expression "from their Lord" (مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ) is an allusion to the enveloping of mercy and the encompassing of divine affection and kindness.

His saying: **كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ فِي النَّارِ** "Are they like those who are immortal in the Fire?" This is a comparison with one side suppressed, meaning: Is he who enters the Paradise of which this is the description like him who is immortal in the Fire and whose drink is water of intense heat that cuts their bowels and the internal organs in their bellies when they are made to drink it? They are only made to drink it while being coerced, as in His saying: **وَسُقُوا ماءً حَمِيماً فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعاءَهُمْ** "and are given to drink boiling water that cuts up their bowels" (47:15). It has been said that His saying: **كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ** "like those who are immortal..." etc., is an elucidation of His saying in the previous verse: **كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ** "like those to whom [the evil of their deeds] has been made to seem fair..." etc., but this is as you see [unlikely].

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** "That is because they loathed what Allah has sent down," Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "They loathed what Allah sent down regarding the right of Ali (peace be upon him)."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **كَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ** "like those to whom the evil of their deeds has been made to seem fair," it is said: "They are the hypocrites." This is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

I say: It is possible that both narrations are instances of "conceptual application" (الجري) — *the application of a verse to a specific instance that fits its general meaning*.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **كَمَنْ هُوَ خالِدٌ فِي النَّارِ وَسُقُوا ماءً حَمِيماً فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعاءَهُمْ** "Are they like those who are immortal in the Fire and are given to drink boiling water that cuts up their bowels?" he said: "He who is in this described Paradise is not like him who is in this Fire, just as the enemy of Allah is not like His friend."
